content: The analysis employs a dual-design approach combining cross-sectional PD prevalence analysis (survey-weighted logistic regression) with prospective mortality analysis (survey-weighted Cox proportional hazards regression linked to the National Death Index). Six pooled NHANES cycles (2007-2018) provide a large nationally representative sample (n=34,133). The Composite Dietary Antioxidant Index (CDAI) — a validated composite score summing standardized intakes of six dietary antioxidants (vitamins A, C, E, carotenoids, selenium, zinc) — serves as the primary exposure. CDAI is analyzed both as a categorical variable (tertiles: Q1 <-1.07, Q2 -1.07 to 1.74, Q3 >1.74) and as a continuous variable via restricted cubic spline (RCS) regression to characterize nonlinear dose-response relationships. PD is identified through prescription of PD-specific medications (carbidopa, levodopa, entacapone, amantadine, pramipexole, bromocriptine). Three progressive adjustment models are used: unadjusted (Model 1), demographics + comorbidities (Model 2), and full model adding metabolic biomarkers (Model 3). Complex survey design is handled using NHANES sampling weights, PSU, and strata.
keywords: Dual design: cross-sectional (logistic) + prospective (Cox PH with NDI linkage)
Six pooled NHANES cycles (2007-2018), n=34,133
Composite Dietary Antioxidant Index (CDAI) from 6 antioxidants
Restricted cubic spline regression for nonlinear dose-response
PD identification via prescription medications (medication-based algorithm)
Progressive model adjustment strategy (3 models)

content: Among 34,133 adults (510 PD, 33,623 non-PD), higher CDAI was significantly associated with lower PD prevalence in the fully adjusted model: Q2 vs Q1 OR 0.70 (95% CI: 0.51-0.96, p=0.027), Q3 vs Q1 OR 0.72 (95% CI: 0.54-0.98, p=0.035). For all-cause mortality among PD patients, fully adjusted Cox regression showed: Q2 vs Q1 HR 0.56 (95% CI: 0.35-0.89, p=0.015), Q3 vs Q1 HR 0.53 (95% CI: 0.31-0.90, p=0.018) — indicating approximately 47% lower mortality risk in the highest CDAI tertile. Restricted cubic spline analysis identified a nonlinear relationship with a turning point at CDAI = 0.471 for PD prevalence. A mortality threshold of CDAI < 0.527 was identified as significantly increasing all-cause mortality risk in PD patients. Subgroup analyses showed the protective effect was most pronounced in individuals over age 60, smokers, and those with hypertension.
keywords: PD prevalence: Q3 vs Q1 OR 0.72 (0.54-0.98) fully adjusted
PD mortality: Q3 vs Q1 HR 0.53 (0.31-0.90) fully adjusted
RCS turning point: CDAI = 0.471 for PD prevalence
Mortality threshold: CDAI < 0.527 increases all-cause mortality in PD
~30% lower PD prevalence in middle/highest CDAI tertiles
Subgroup: pronounced protection in age >60, smokers, hypertensives

content: The study uses progressive model adjustment: Model 1 (unadjusted), Model 2 (age, gender, hypertension, diabetes), and Model 3 (adding fasting glucose, ALT, creatinine/BMI, triglycerides, total cholesterol). This sequential approach allows readers to evaluate how metabolic confounders attenuate or strengthen associations. For mortality analysis, Model 6 adds BMI instead of creatinine. Subgroup analyses by age, gender, smoking, hypertension, diabetes, and alcohol consumption serve as informal tests of effect modification and assess robustness across strata. The study identifies that CDAI's protective effect is amplified in subgroups with higher oxidative stress burden (smokers, hypertensives, elderly). This is biologically plausible: smoking generates reactive oxygen species, hypertension causes endothelial oxidative damage, and aging reduces endogenous antioxidant defenses — all conditions where exogenous dietary antioxidants may have the greatest marginal benefit. A key limitation is that PD was identified by medication use only, potentially missing undiagnosed or untreated PD cases.
keywords: Progressive model adjustment: demographics → metabolic biomarkers
Subgroup analysis across 6 stratification variables
Effect modification strongest in high-oxidative-stress subgroups
PD case identification limitation: medication-based only
Residual confounding from unmeasured genetic and environmental factors
Dietary recall measurement error from self-reported 24-hour recalls

content: The study provides the first evidence that a composite dietary antioxidant index is inversely associated with both PD prevalence and all-cause mortality in PD patients in a nationally representative U.S. population. The CDAI threshold of 0.527 provides a clinically actionable target: PD patients maintaining CDAI above this level may have significantly lower mortality risk. The finding that antioxidant protection is most pronounced in individuals with high oxidative stress burden (age >60, smokers, hypertensive) suggests targeted nutritional interventions could be most effective in these subgroups. The composite index approach (CDAI) captures synergistic effects of multiple antioxidants rather than examining individual nutrients in isolation — more closely reflecting real dietary patterns. The authors note that the observational design precludes causal inference, dietary intake was assessed by 24-hour recall (subject to measurement error and recall bias), and PD was identified through medication records only, potentially underestimating true PD prevalence by missing undiagnosed or untreated cases.
keywords: First CDAI-PD prevalence and CDAI-PD mortality study in NHANES
CDAI threshold 0.527 as clinically actionable target for PD patients
Targeted nutritional intervention for high-oxidative-stress subgroups
Composite antioxidant approach captures dietary synergy
Observational design limits causal inference
Medication-based PD identification may undercount true prevalence

content: Published in Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience (IF ~4.1), a peer-reviewed open-access journal indexed in PubMed/PMC. The study follows STROBE guidelines for cross-sectional and cohort studies. Participant selection from NHANES 2007-2018 is documented with a clear exclusion flowchart (119,609 initial → 34,133 final). All odds ratios and hazard ratios are reported with 95% confidence intervals and p-values across three progressive adjustment models. CDAI calculation methodology is explicitly described following the validated Wright/Zhou standardization approach. Restricted cubic spline plots visualize the nonlinear dose-response. Subgroup forest plots display stratified results. Study limitations including cross-sectional component, self-reported dietary data, medication-based PD ascertainment, and potential residual confounding are acknowledged. The study is open access (CC BY 4.0), enhancing reproducibility.
keywords: STROBE adherence for cross-sectional + cohort design
Published in Frontiers in Aging Neuroscience (IF ~4.1, open access)
Exclusion flowchart from 119,609 to 34,133 participants
All ORs and HRs with 95% CI across 3 models
RCS dose-response visualization
Open access (CC BY 4.0) for full reproducibility
